title: scenario_equipment_failure_during_incident
description: Critical equipment fails during an active enforcement incident. Tests officer improvisation, safety protocols, communication obligations, and the duty to report equipment failures under police service policy.

timeline.title: Dispatch to Disturbance
timeline.description: Officer is dispatched to a subject causing a disturbance, described as agitated and throwing objects.

timeline.title: Contact and Threat Escalation
timeline.description: Officer contacts the subject. The subject is holding a broken bottle and advancing.

timeline.title: CEW Deployed — Malfunction
timeline.description: Officer deploys conducted energy weapon. The CEW fires but the subject is not incapacitated — one probe missed.

timeline.title: Subject Controlled
timeline.description: Officer deploys OC spray as secondary option. Subject is controlled with backup assistance.

timeline.title: Post-Incident Scene
timeline.description: Subject is in custody. Officer discovers their body-worn camera was not recording during the incident.

timeline.title: Supervisor Arrives
timeline.description: Sergeant responds and begins equipment failure reporting process.

node-1.title: CEW Malfunction During Lethal-Threat Encounter
node-1.situation: You are responding alone to a disturbance. A male subject is holding a broken bottle and advancing toward you, saying he will cut you. You issue verbal commands — the subject does not comply. You deploy your CEW. One probe connects but the second probe misses, and the subject continues advancing. He is now approximately 3 metres away with the broken bottle raised. Backup is 90 seconds away. You have OC spray and a firearm available.
node-1.question: What is your use-of-force response when your primary intermediate weapon has partially failed?

options.label: A
options.text: Your force options must escalate with the threat: the CEW has partially failed and the subject continues to advance with a weapon. Your immediate response should be: (1) Create maximum distance — move backward while issuing commands; (2) Deploy OC spray as your next available intermediate option — a subject at 3 metres closing with a bladed weapon is within the 'reactionary gap'; (3) If the subject closes further and presents an imminent lethal threat (within striking distance with the broken bottle), your use-of-force analysis under Criminal Code s.34 and your force continuum may justify drawing your firearm as the threat has reached potentially lethal; (4) Broadcast your situation continuously — 'Weapons call — [location] — officer deploying OC'; (5) Do not reach for the CEW again if one probe is not properly connected — re-drive cycles will not be effective.

options.label: B
options.text: Re-holster the CEW and attempt to physically restrain the subject with your hands while the weapon is raised.

options.label: C
options.text: Wait for backup before doing anything further — deploying additional force without a second officer present is too risky.

node-2.title: Body-Worn Camera Failure Discovered
node-2.situation: The subject is in custody. Backup has arrived. While preparing your incident report, you discover your body-worn camera shows a 'recording error' indicator — it was not recording during the critical part of the encounter. The CEW deployment and the entire confrontation with the broken bottle were not captured. Your use-of-force report will need to describe events that have no video documentation.
node-2.question: How do you handle the discovery of the body-worn camera failure?

options.label: A
options.text: Report the BWC failure immediately and transparently: (1) Advise your supervisor now — a use-of-force incident without BWC documentation requires supervisor notification and additional scrutiny; (2) Do not alter or delay your use-of-force report — write an accurate, detailed account of the exact sequence of events including the CEW deployment attempt and its partial failure; (3) Preserve the BWC device as-is — do not attempt to recover data yourself or tamper with the device; (4) Complete a separate equipment deficiency report for the BWC malfunction; (5) Identify any independent witnesses or other officers on scene who may have observed any portion of the incident. Transparent reporting of equipment failures is a professional obligation — concealing or minimising the failure would be a serious breach of integrity.

options.label: B
options.text: Do not mention the BWC failure — the incident is resolved and raising documentation gaps may create unnecessary scrutiny of your actions.

options.label: C
options.text: Review the BWC footage to confirm it failed before reporting anything — you may have made an error and the footage may exist.

node-3.title: Equipment Deficiency Report and Accountability
node-3.situation: Your supervisor has documented the CEW malfunction and BWC failure. The CEW is being sent for inspection. Your sergeant asks how you want to handle the use-of-force report given the absence of video documentation, and suggests you 'keep it brief so there is less to scrutinise.'
node-3.question: How do you respond to the sergeant's suggestion to keep the report brief?

options.label: A
options.text: Respectfully but firmly decline the sergeant's suggestion. A use-of-force report must be complete and accurate — the absence of BWC footage makes thorough documentation more important, not less. Your report should detail: your exact position when the subject was encountered, the subject's behaviour and actions, the specific verbal commands issued, the CEW deployment (including the probe-miss failure), the transition to OC spray, and all subsequent actions. A brief report in a use-of-force case with no video creates significant risk if the incident is reviewed by the SIU, a civilian complaint body, or a court. If the sergeant is pressuring you to underreport, note that conversation and bring it to your union representative.

options.label: B
options.text: Agree with the sergeant — a brief report is less likely to attract a civilian complaint or SIU review.

options.label: C
options.text: Submit the report and then speak to the sergeant about their suggestion — completing the report first keeps things professional.

node-4.title: Scenario Complete
node-4.situation: Your detailed use-of-force report and equipment deficiency reports were submitted accurately. The CEW inspection found a manufacturing defect in the probe cartridge. The BWC failure was traced to a firmware issue affecting that batch of cameras — all devices were inspected and updated. Your thorough reporting meant that when a civilian complaint was filed two weeks later, the investigation had a complete factual record to rely on. The complaint was resolved in your favour. Your sergeant's suggestion was addressed by the duty inspector as an integrity concern. Equipment failures are inevitable — the obligation to report them accurately and completely protects officers, the public, and the integrity of every use-of-force incident.
node-4.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
